title: Enthalpy of Combustion of Alcohols
method: Weigh the spirit burner containing the alcohol
Measure a known volume of water into a copper calorimeter
Record the initial temperature of the water
Light the spirit burner and heat the water
Extinguish the flame after a suitable temperature rise (~20°C)
Record the final temperature and reweigh the spirit burner

variables.independent: Type of alcohol (methanol, ethanol, propan-1-ol, etc.)

safety: Flammable alcohols — keep away from open flames when not in use
Use a heat mat under the burner
calculations: q = mcΔT where m = mass of water (g), c = 4.18 J/g/°C, ΔT = temperature change. Divide by moles of alcohol burned to get enthalpy of combustion.
examTips: Explain why experimental values are lower than data book values (heat loss, incomplete combustion)
Suggest improvements: use a bomb calorimeter, add a lid, use draught shield

title: Acid-Base Titration
method: Rinse the burette with the acid, then fill and record the initial reading
Use a pipette to transfer 25.0 cm³ of alkali into a conical flask
Add 2-3 drops of indicator (e.g., phenolphthalein)
Add acid from the burette slowly, swirling the flask
Add dropwise near the endpoint until the colour changes permanently
Record the final burette reading and calculate the titre
Repeat until concordant results (within 0.10 cm³)
safety: Wear goggles — acids and alkalis are corrosive
Use a pipette filler, never pipette by mouth
calculations: Use concordant titres (mean of values within 0.10 cm³). n = cV/1000. Use mole ratios from balanced equation to find unknown concentration.
examTips: Always read the meniscus at eye level for the burette
Concordant means within 0.10 cm³ — discard anomalous results
Explain why you use a white tile (to see the colour change clearly)

title: Testing for Halide Ions
method: Dissolve the sample in deionised water
Add dilute nitric acid (to remove carbonate/sulfate ions that interfere)
Add silver nitrate solution (AgNO₃)
Observe the precipitate colour
To confirm, add dilute then concentrated ammonia
safety: Silver nitrate stains skin — wear gloves
Nitric acid is corrosive — wear goggles
Work in a fume cupboard with concentrated ammonia
calculations: No calculation — qualitative test. Cl⁻ = white precipitate (soluble in dilute NH₃), Br⁻ = cream precipitate (soluble in conc. NH₃), I⁻ = yellow precipitate (insoluble in NH₃).
examTips: Always add nitric acid FIRST to remove interfering ions
Know the ammonia solubility test to distinguish between halides

title: Measuring Rate of Reaction
method: Set up the apparatus (gas syringe method or disappearing cross)
Measure and record volumes/masses of reactants
Mix reactants and start timing immediately
Record volume of gas produced (or time for cross to disappear) at regular intervals
Plot a graph of volume/time or 1/time against the variable

variables.independent: Concentration of acid (or temperature, surface area, catalyst)
variables.dependent: Volume of gas produced per unit time (or time for cross to disappear)

safety: Ensure gas syringe is clamped securely
If using HCl with marble chips, ensure good ventilation (CO₂)
calculations: Rate = volume of gas / time. Initial rate from gradient of tangent at t=0. Mean rate = total gas / total time.
examTips: A steeper initial gradient means a faster rate
The total volume of gas at the end is the same if the limiting reagent amount is unchanged
Draw a tangent to find the initial rate — examiners check your tangent technique

title: Preparation of an Organic Liquid (Ester)
method: Mix the alcohol and carboxylic acid in a pear-shaped flask
Add a few drops of concentrated sulfuric acid as catalyst
Set up reflux apparatus and heat gently for 15-20 minutes
Allow to cool, then distil to collect the ester
Wash the distillate with sodium carbonate solution to remove excess acid
Dry with anhydrous calcium chloride, then redistil for purity

variables.independent: Type of alcohol / carboxylic acid used
variables.dependent: Yield and boiling point of ester produced

safety: Concentrated sulfuric acid is very corrosive — add carefully
Esters and alcohols are flammable — no naked flames near apparatus
Use anti-bumping granules to prevent bumping during reflux
calculations: Theoretical yield from moles of limiting reagent. Actual yield from mass of product collected. Percentage yield = (actual / theoretical) × 100%.
examTips: Explain why reflux is used (to prevent loss of volatile reactants while maintaining reaction temperature)
Know the difference between reflux and distillation
Explain each purification step: washing removes water-soluble impurities, drying removes water, redistilling improves purity

title: Colorimetry and Calibration Curves
method: Prepare a series of standard solutions of known concentration
Set the colorimeter to the appropriate filter (complementary colour)
Calibrate with a blank (deionised water) to zero absorbance
Measure the absorbance of each standard solution
Plot a calibration curve (absorbance vs concentration)
Measure the absorbance of the unknown solution and read off the concentration
safety: Some coloured solutions may be irritants — wear gloves
Handle cuvettes by the frosted sides only (fingerprints affect readings)
calculations: Use Beer-Lambert law: A = εcl. Read unknown concentration from calibration graph. Ensure readings are within the linear range.
examTips: Choose a filter that gives the complementary colour to the solution
Always calibrate with a blank first
If the unknown has a higher absorbance than your standards, dilute and multiply the result
